primary_diagnosis.name: Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (decompensated type 2 diabetes) with hyperosmolar coma and hypovolaemic shock

pathophysiology_summary: Relative insulin deficiency in type 2 diabetes, compounded here by a week of missed oral hypoglycaemics, allows blood glucose to climb unchecked (meter reads above its 33.3 mmol/L ceiling). Severe hyperglycaemia drives an osmotic diuresis over days to weeks, producing massive polyuria and profound total-body water and electrolyte loss. Because some endogenous insulin persists, ketogenesis is suppressed (near-normal ketones 0.4, no Kussmaul respirations or acetone breath) — this is the key distinction from DKA. The result is extreme hyperosmolality with progressive dehydration and haemoconcentration, causing altered consciousness that can range from confusion to coma, and — because the intravascular volume is depleted and the blood is hyperviscous — hypovolaemic/hypoperfusion shock. Hyperosmolality and hyperviscosity can produce focal neurological signs (here, transient right-arm weakness) that mimic acute stroke without a primary vascular occlusion, and raise thromboembolic risk. Untreated, HHS carries high mortality from cardiovascular collapse, cerebral injury and thrombosis. Definitive treatment is cautious, protocol-driven intravenous fluid resuscitation to correct the osmolar deficit slowly (avoiding cerebral oedema), with insulin introduced carefully after volume replacement — hospital/ICU care that cannot be delivered via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Known diabetic, weeks of polyuria/polydipsia, progressive decline to unrousable, and profound dehydration — the classic HHS trajectory.
Transient focal right-arm weakness with reduced consciousness could signal a primary vascular event — must be actively excluded, but does not explain the systemic dehydration and unreadable glucose.
Sepsis can cause hypotension and altered mentation and is a common precipitant of HHS; afebrile here but cannot be excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage a metabolic emergency should already be the leading concern in a diabetic who is unrousable and dehydrated; anchoring solely on stroke is the trap.

differential.evidence_basis: Bedside glucose above meter ceiling (>33.3), low ketones (0.4), no Kussmaul breathing, profound dehydration and coma — hyperosmolar, non-ketotic picture.
Near-normal ketones (0.4) and absence of Kussmaul/acetone breath make significant ketoacidosis unlikely — this is the hyperosmolar, not the ketoacidotic, hyperglycaemic emergency.
Focal sign was transient and is better explained by hyperosmolality; still requires hospital imaging to exclude, but is not the primary driver.
Can cause coma, but the specific two-week history of osmotic-diuresis-driven dehydration preceding the coma points to hyperglycaemic hyperosmolality.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The low ketones + no Kussmaul distinguish HHS from DKA; the transient, resolving focal sign in the context of extreme hyperglycaemia argues against a primary stroke as the cause of coma.

differential.diagnosis: Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state with hyperosmolar coma and hypovolaemic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Life-threatening metabolic emergency requiring immediate emergency transport and hospital/ICU fluid-and-insulin resuscitation; definitive osmolality/electrolytes confirm in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm osmolality or electrolytes — the correct action is emergency escalation on pattern recognition, not remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Unrousable / markedly reduced conscious level (progressive over 48 hours to coma)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Coma indicates a critical, immediately life-threatening derangement; airway is at risk and emergency resuscitation is required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Airway compromise and death from unprotected airway or ongoing metabolic collapse.

red_flags.description: Profound dehydration (sunken eyes, parched mucous membranes) with circulatory compromise
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extreme total-body water depletion with impending or actual hypovolaemic shock — time-critical for intravenous resuscitation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible shock, end-organ hypoperfusion and cardiovascular collapse.

red_flags.description: Transient focal neurological deficit (right-arm weakness) — stroke mimic from hyperosmolality
red_flags.clinical_significance: Focal signs mandate emergency assessment (to exclude stroke and because thromboembolic risk is raised), but must not anchor the clinician away from the metabolic emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Either a missed stroke or, more likely here, delayed treatment of the underlying hyperosmolar catastrophe by over-focusing on a vascular cause.

red_flags.description: Bedside capillary glucose above the meter ceiling (>33.3 mmol/L) with low ketones
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extreme hyperglycaemia without ketosis is the biochemical signature of HHS; patient-obtained and unverified but strongly corroborates a hyperglycaemic emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise a hyperglycaemic emergency and under-triage of a dying patient.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Fixating on the transient focal arm weakness and reduced consciousness as a primary stroke, missing that the deficit is a hyperosmolar stroke-mimic and that the immediate threat is a metabolic emergency.

Assuming any hyperglycaemic diabetic emergency is DKA; the low ketones and absent Kussmaul breathing here define HHS, which has a different (slower, cautious) fluid/insulin approach and higher mortality.
Trying to manage at home — giving oral fluids, food, sugar or insulin to an unconscious person who cannot swallow — which risks aspiration and delays definitive care.
Attempting to instruct an insulin dose remotely; insulin in HHS is a cautious in-hospital step after volume resuscitation, never a telehealth instruction, and never a synthesised dose.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure and heart rate (source clinician measured BP 85/50, HR 120 — indicating shock)
Skin turgor / tenting assessed by hand
Auscultated respiratory pattern and confirmation of absent Kussmaul respirations
Serum osmolality, sodium, potassium, glucose, renal function, venous/arterial blood gas
CT/MRI brain to formally exclude stroke
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance for a suspected hyperglycaemic emergency with reduced consciousness and shock; place the patient in the recovery (lateral) position to protect the airway; give NOTHING by mouth (no fluids, food, sugar or insulin) as she cannot swallow; stay on the line, unlock the door, and gather her medications and blood-sugar meter for the paramedics.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising a life-threatening emergency (the pattern is unambiguous); the real risks are anchoring on stroke, mislabelling as DKA, or attempting home/oral management instead of immediate escalation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian Diabetes Society — Hyperglycaemic Emergencies in Adults (2022)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: HHS is defined by marked hyperglycaemia and hyperosmolality without significant ketoacidosis; management is emergency hospital care with cautious IV fluid replacement and delayed, careful insulin — not home management.

guidelines_referenced.reference: JBDS-IP — Management of the Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State in Adults (2022)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Correct osmolality slowly to avoid cerebral oedema; anticipate high thrombotic risk and cardiovascular instability; treat as a medical emergency requiring inpatient/critical-care management.
